system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 2: Musical Scales & Intervals
Pages 26-46 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

    Chapter 2                                                                                          4668




    Musical Scales & Intervals
    Diatonic Scale:
    The diatonic scale is a sequence of 7 musical notes organized in half steps and whole steps.
    It is based on the 7 “natural” notes: C, D, E, F, G, A, B. The half steps are between the notes:
    B, C and E, F. If we consider the repetition of the "C" at the end of it, we will have the
    musical interval of "octave" between the two "C's".

    This sequence of notes (C, D, E, F, G, A, B), brings a "happy and joyful" sound that we call "C"
    natural major scale or major scale. It is organized using the pattern:



    • W-W-H-W-W-W-H • (W = whole tone / H = half tone).


    It can be ascending decending as in presented below:


         ascending
                                    68                    descending

     & 44 œ     œ    œ    œ
                         46     œ      œ     œ     œ       œ      œ      œ      œ     œ   œ   œ   œ

    We can start the scale from any note, but the sound of the sequence will not be the same,
    because the scale pattern will be changed. For example: The "C" major scale pattern is:
    -W - W - H - W - W - W - H, but if we start it from the second note (D) the pattern would
    change to - W - H - W - W - W - H - W.


    Attention:
    In the following chapters we will learn about other scales, and the necessary changes to
    create major scales starting from other notes.


7                             2019 © Jupiter Musical Group Ltd. All Rights Reserved


Minor Scale (Relative Scale)
                                                                                                    4668




The natural minor scale, also called relative scale, is the major scale itself started from the
6th degree. Therefore, if we have a "C major" scale, the minor scale will be "A minor".

We consider that both scales have a kind of relationship and connection between each
other, because both are composed of natural notes and have opposing sonorities that are
complementary (major = happy and minor = sad). The relative scale interval pattern is:

•W-H-W-W-H-T-T•

It can be ascending and decending as in presented below:

    ascending                                        descending
                                œ     œ       œ      œ        œ     œ      œ
&œ        œ     œ      œ                                                            œ   œ   œ   œ


Chromatic Scale:

The chromatic scale consists of 12 half steps. In other words, it contains all 12 notes that

                                68
exist in western music (C, C#, D, D#, E, F, F#, G, G#, A, A#, B). This scale brings us a
hybrid sound, which means that it is not happy nor sad. We can say that the chromatic
scale is in constant motion.
                     46

     Devices for visualizing notes on Fretboard
                                                                                                    4668




     Step 1:
     Memorize the names of the open string notes.

     Step 2:
     Memorize the names of the natural notes on the 6th and 5th strings (Note: "natural note"
     means notes without any accidentals). Notes containing accidentals will consequently be
     visualized. Remember that the 1st and 6th strings are the same (E).

     Step 3:
     Notes belonging to the 4th, 3rd string can be visualized using the interval of octaves from
     the 5th and 6th strings. For example:
     • the octave from a 6th string note will give us
       the same note (1 octave higher) on the 4th string.
     • the octave from a 5th string note will give us
       the same note (1 octave higher) on the 3th string.

     Step 4:
                                   68
     The second string notes can be visualized using an octave from the 4th string. So, before
     trying to visualize the 2nd string notes, make sure you know the 4th string notes very well.

     Step 5:
                          46
     Memorize the notes from major and minor scales and also arpeggios in many different
     keys. We advise you to memorize them in the following natural keys : C , G , D , A , E , B.


     Attention:
     There are many different ways to visualize the notes, each one of you can have your own
     way to do it. However, in this book we are giving you some special tools that we know
     can help you to Pind your own path.

     When we are learning how to play an instrument one of the most important things to know is
                                                                                                                    4668




     where the notes are, visualizing them by intervals. The guitar has an advantage over the other
     instruments because we can learn things by shapes. In other words, it does not matter the key
     we are in, the shape will be the same, we only have to move from one fret to another (a major
     3rd will always be in the same position and so on). However, we must be careful when learning
     a scale, because if we memorize it only by shape without knowing the intervals, eventually we
     will have to come back and learn the intervals, otherwise it would be impossible to go to the
     next step. Therefore, both must be learned at the same time. The secret to visualizing the guitar
     fretboard is to learn everything having two guide points:
     a) guide note on the 6th string
     b) guide note on the 5th string
     That guide note is what we call ROOT or 1, and all the intervals we play will be related with
     it, even the most distant ones. Below we have the interval numbers of each scale:

     Classification of Intervals:                                                                                              4668




     Intervals may be classified as follows:

     a) melodic or harmonic                                               d) diatonic or chromatic
     b) simple or compound                                                e) ascending or descending
     c) consonant or dissonant                                            f) enharmonic


     a) MELODIC & HARMONIC INTERVALS : Melodic intervals are notes played one after the
     other, and harmonic intervals are notes played simultaneously.


                 harmonic interval                                                             melodic interval

      &                      ˙˙                                                                               ˙
                                                                              &                ˙


     b) SIMPLE & COMPOUND INTERVALS : Simple intervals are intervals within one octave,
     and compound intervals are those separated by more than one octave.

                  simple major 3rd                                                       compound major 3rd
                                                                                                     w
      &                  ww                                                   &
                                                                                                     w
                                           68
                              46
     c) CONSONANT & DISSONANT INTERVALS : Consonant intervals are those considered
     stable (relaxing feel to the ears), and dissonant are those considered unstable (tension feeling to
     the ears). It is always good to remember that what was considered dissonant 100 years ago is not
     necessarily considered dissonant today, because it is just a matter of getting used to the sound.
     So dissonant and consonant are not a fixed concept that can not evolve, that is the reason why it
     has changed since the early days of music. See below a table of what we will consider consonant
     and dissonant intervals:                                                               P = perfect
                                                                                                              M = major

a guiding point to make the connections and relations between the notes. So in order to
transform this maze into a map, let's use two guiding points, one on the 5th string and
another on the 6th string. All the notes we play will be related with one of them depending
on which guiding point we use. It is important to understand that the guiding points will
help us to have a vertical visualization of the fretboard, which can have the same intervals
to the right or to the left side of it. See below:

Intervals Study 1:                                                                                    4668




Knowing the intervals on paper is not enough. It is necessary to learn them by singing. In
order to make that easier, let's use some songs as references. The process is quite simple, we will
learn the beginning of each song that contains each one of the 12 ascending intervals, as well the
12 descending intervals. Therefore, there will be 24 songs (24 intervals).



Ascending Intervals:
1) Minor Second = Isn't She Lovely (Stevie Wonder)
2) Major Second = Happy Birthday (P. Hill / M. J. Hill )
3) Minor Third = Sugar (Stanley Turrentine)
4) Major Third = I Can't Get Started (Vernon Duke)
5) Perfect Fourth = Someday My Prince Will Come (Frank Churchill)
6) Aug Fourth/Dim Fifth = Maria - West Side Story (L. Bernstein)
7) Perfect Fifth = Star Wars (John Williams)
8) Aug Fifth/ Minor Sixth = Manhã de Carnaval (L. Bonfá)
9) Major Sixth = Take the “A” Train (Billy Strayhorn)
10) Minor Seventh = Star Trek theme (M. Giacchino)
11) Major Seventh = Super Man (J. Williams)
12) Perfect Octave = Somewhere Over the Rainbow (Harold Arlen)

     Intervals Study 2:
                                                                                                           4668




     After learning the songs of each interval, it is time to check what we really know. So, let's learn
     seven basic studies:


     1) Use a harmonic instrument, preferably a piano. Play two random notes within the same octave
     and sing the interval to be studied. Study only one at a time and repeat many times.


     2) Now do the same study alternating types of intervals. Ex. M2, M3, P4.


     3) Study with a partner. Play random intervals on the piano, so that your partner can respond
     singing as fast as he can. Then reverse the roles. Preferably use a metronome and arrange
     between yourselves how many measures each of you should respond to.


     4) Pick a piano note to be the root that will guide us. Sing the interval to be studied keeping the
     piano guide note always present (we can play the "guide note" again to reinforce the pitch). This
     study aims to make us feel the "power" of each note.


     5) Again, choose a piano note to be the root that will guide us, but this time singing a simple
                                    68
     song over it. Try to feel all the intervals of the song, and try to use folk songs that are always
     simpler.

                           46
     6) Now try to sing the 3 scales we have learned previously, always using a piano note to be the
     root (choose a comfortable pitch to sing).


     a) Diatonic Scale
     b) Minor Scale
     c) Chromatic Scale


     7) Now study harmonic intervals with a partner. Start repeating the same interval but in many
     different keys. As soon as you are comfortable with all the harmonic intervals separately, make it
     more difficult by playing the intervals randomly in different keys.

(lower note to higher note):
To facilitate the visualization of the interval on the staff, there are a few tricks we need to know.
The table below shows exactly how they work:

Very Important:
                                68
                      46
2nd : note that goes from line to space or note that goes from space to line.

3rd : note that goes from one line to the next, or note that goes from one space to the next.

4th : note that goes from line to space, skipping a line, or note that goes from space to line
skipping a space.
5th : note that goes from line to line, skipping a line, or note that goes from space to space
skipping a space.
6th : note that goes from line to space skipping two lines, or note that goes from space to line
skipping two spaces.
7th : note that goes from line to line skipping two lines, or note that goes from space to space
skipping two spaces.

     Descending Intervals in the Staff                                                           4668




     (higher note to lower note):

     It is important to understand the relationship between ascending intervals and descending
     intervals. Example:



             C                                           C
                    ⇑ minor 3 rd                                                  6 th
     &       w
             w
             ↑
                                               &         w
                                                         w
                                                         ↑
                                                                   ⇓      major

             A                                           A
             ↓                                           ↓


     • Therefore, we conclude that:
     1) Up a Minor 2nd = Down a Major 7th

     2) Up a Major 2nd = Down a Minor 7th

     3 Up a Minor 3rd = Down a Major 6th

     4) Up a Major 3rd = Down a Minor 6th

                                 68
     5) Up a Perfect 4th = Down a Perfect 5th


                        46
     6) Up a Augmented 4th (diminished 5th) = Down a Augmented 4th (no changes)

     7) Up a Perfect 5th = Down a Perfect 4th

     8) Up a Augmented 5th (minor 6th) = Down a Major 3rd

     9) Up a Major 6th = Down a Minor 3rd

     10) Up a Minor 7th = Down a Major 2nd

     11) Up a Major 7th = Down a Minor 2nd

     12) Up a Octave = Down a Octave ( no changes)
